nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical coral reefs with hard substrate, strong currents, and high light exposure; often found on exposed reef crests and upper slopes

decorPreferences: powerhead guards — anemone will wander and climb
wide open rockwork for spreading oral disc
no sharp or rough surfaces that can damage column

diet.notes: The Ritteri Anemone meets most of its energy needs through zooxanthellae — symbiotic algae that live within its tissues and photosynthesize — but it also actively captures prey with its tentacles. Weekly target-feeding with silversides, krill, raw shrimp, or other meaty marine foods is generally recommended. Hosting clownfish contribute additional nutrients through waste and mucus exchange. Overfeeding should be avoided, as bloating can be fatal.

goodWith: reef-safe fish that will not pick at tentacles
Lysmata cleaner shrimp (with caution — may be consumed)
avoidWith: other anemone species — territorial and will sting to the death
SPS and LPS corals — potent sting damages neighbors
powerheads and circulation pumps without guards — anemone wanders and ingests into impellers
angelfish and butterflyfish — tend to nip tentacles

summary: The Ritteri Anemone is a large clownfish host widely considered one of the most challenging anemones in the marine hobby. It demands intense lighting, pristine water chemistry, and an experienced keeper — captive mortality rates are notoriously high.
careNotes:
The Ritteri Anemone (Heteractis magnifica), also sold as the Magnificent Anemone, is generally considered one of the most difficult anemones in the marine hobby and carries an expert-only designation from most major marine retailers. Captive mortality is notoriously high, and this species is not recommended for beginners or intermediate keepers. Only attempt it with a mature reef system (at least 12 months established) and prior experience keeping anemones.

Key care requirements:

• Lighting: Intense lighting is essential — generally advised to target 250–450 PAR. This anemone tends to wander upward toward the light source and will often climb to powerhead height in search of adequate illumination. Gradual light acclimation over several weeks is recommended for new arrivals, starting at lower intensities to prevent bleaching.

• Wandering behavior: Ritteri anemones have been known to roam extensively, even in well-established tanks. This creates a serious powerhead hazard; all circulation pumps must be covered with foam guards or similar protection, or the anemone risks being pulled into the impeller. An open aquascape with no obstructions gives the anemone room to settle and reduces risk.

• Flow: Moderate, intermittent flow is generally recommended. Strong direct laminar flow can damage tentacles; alternating gyre-type flow or wave-making is preferred. The anemone should gently sway, but tentacles should not stream or fold flat.

• Water quality: Pristine water conditions are required. Nitrates should generally be kept below 10 ppm and phosphates below 0.05 ppm. Ammonia and nitrite must be zero. Newly arrived specimens typically require several months to fully acclimate and are most vulnerable during this period.

• Clownfish hosting: Keeping compatible clownfish (particularly A. ocellaris, A. percula, or A. perideraion) has been reported to improve acclimation and long-term health. The clownfish appear to stimulate the anemone's activity and contribute nutrients through waste. H. magnifica is a primary natural host for these species.

• Feeding: Target-feed weekly with silversides, krill, or pieces of raw marine fish or shrimp. The anemone captures prey with its tentacles; spot-feeding with a turkey baster or tongs near the oral disc tends to produce the best results. Uneaten food that remains trapped will degrade water quality.

• Captive size: Wild specimens can reach 1 m in oral disc diameter; in aquaria they generally reach 30–50 cm, occasionally approaching 75 cm in large, well-maintained systems.

• Reproduction in captivity: Asexual reproduction via fission (where the column splits into two separate individuals) has been observed in well-established captive specimens, particularly when hosting clownfish.
breedingNotes: Deliberate breeding of the Ritteri Anemone is not practiced in the hobby. In the wild, H. magnifica reproduces both sexually — releasing eggs and sperm into the water column simultaneously so fertilization occurs in open water — and asexually through a process called fission, in which the body column splits down the middle to produce two separate, genetically identical individuals. Asexual fission has been documented in captive specimens and tends to occur more frequently when hosting clownfish are present, which appear to stimulate the behavior. Once the two halves have fully separated, each individual can be maintained independently.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Ritteri Anemone (Expert Only)
primary.notes: Primary retail source. Temperature 72–78°F (22–25.6°C), SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12. Expert Only designation. No minimum tank size stated on listing.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Radianthus magnifica (Heteractis magnifica)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Stichodactylidae, Order: Actiniaria), 2023 reclassification to Radianthus magnifica, native range (Red Sea to Polynesia, southern Japan to Australia), oral disc size 20–100 cm, depth surface to 40 m, list of 12 hosted clownfish species, and reproduction modes.

additional.name: Reeflings — Heteractis magnifica Complete Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 22–26°C, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, tank minimum 200 L (53 gal). Care level 'moderate to difficult'. Temperature slightly lower than SaltWaterFish; temperature range averaged across sources.

additional.name: SaltWaterFish.com — Ritteri Anemone (Radiantus magnifica)
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 76–82°F (24.4–27.8°C), SG 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, tank 50 gal (189 L). Expert only. Temperature upper bound (28°C) supported by this source and wild data.

additional.name: Animal Diversity Web — Heteractis magnifica
additional.notes: Used for wild biology: adult disc diameter up to 1 m, depth 1–50 m (average 25 m), wild temperature 24–32°C, native range Red Sea to Samoa, lifespan unknown in wild (some individuals estimated hundreds of years), maximum captive record 80 years. Not used for aquarium parameters.
